meta.title: Ohm's Law — Complete Visual Course
meta.subtitle: 10 scenes · from electrons to real circuits
meta.description: A full educational SVG animation teaching Ohm's Law from first principles through worked examples.

scene1.description: Hook — What is electricity? Electrons moving through a conductor.

props.content: Electricity = directed flow of free electrons

scene2.description: Voltage — the pressure that pushes electrons. Water-pressure analogy.

scene3.description: Current — the rate of electron flow, measured in Amperes.

scene4.description: Resistance — friction that opposes current. Pipe-friction analogy.

scene5.description: Ohm's Law formula revealed: V = I × R with the memory triangle.

scene6.description: Worked example 1: V=12V, R=4Ω → solve for I=3A. Step-by-step.

scene7.description: Voltage-Current proportionality: double V → double I (R constant).

props.content: I ∝ V   (when R is constant — double V, double I)

scene8.description: Complete circuit diagram: battery, resistor, ammeter — electrons flowing.

scene9.description: Real-world applications: phone charger, light bulb, electric heater.

props.content: Ohm's Law is at work in every electrical device you use!

scene10.description: Summary and memory aid — full review of all three quantities and the law.

narration.text: Electricity is the directed flow of electrons through a conductor.
Electrons are tiny negative charges. When they move together, we get electric current.
Voltage is the electrical pressure that drives electrons forward — like water pressure in a pipe.
Higher voltage means more push. Voltage is measured in Volts (V).
Current is how many electrons pass a point per second — the flow rate.
Current is measured in Amperes. One Ampere = 6.24 billion billion electrons per second!
Resistance is the friction that slows electrons down. It's like a narrow pipe opposing water flow.
Resistance is measured in Ohms. More resistance means less current for the same voltage.
Georg Ohm discovered the relationship: Voltage equals Current times Resistance.
Use the triangle: cover the quantity you want to find and read off the formula.
Example: 12 volts, 4 ohms resistance — what is the current?
Use I = V divided by R. That's 12 divided by 4 — giving us 3 Amperes.
When resistance stays fixed, current is proportional to voltage. Double V, double I.
At 6 volts we get 1.5 amps. At 12 volts we get 3 amps. Exactly double — as predicted.
Now let's build a complete circuit: battery, resistor, and ammeter, all connected.
Electrons leave the negative terminal, flow through the resistor, and return to positive.
Ohm's Law is everywhere — your phone charger, light bulbs, and heaters all obey it.
A charger: 5V and 2.5 ohms gives 2 amps. A heater: 240V and 24 ohms gives 10 amps.
To summarise: V is voltage in Volts, I is current in Amps, R is resistance in Ohms.
And Ohm's Law ties them together: V = I × R. Master this, and circuits make sense!
